title: On-Device AI Watcher
subtitle: Public-source tracker of local AI model deployment on phones, tablets, and personal compute — the model families, runtimes, platform stacks, and capability envelope. Quarterly cadence.
scope: Model families with first-class on-device variants (Gemma 4 E-variants, Apple Foundation Models, Gemini Nano, Llama 3.2 / Llama 4 small, Qwen 2.5 small, Phi mini, Granite 3, DeepSeek small). Runtimes that host them (llama.cpp, MLX, Ollama, Core ML, Android AICore, ExecuTorch). Platform stacks that integrate them (Apple Intelligence, Android AICore, Qualcomm AI Engine). Device tiers from flagship phone to Raspberry Pi. Seeded from public materials only — lab blogs, platform launch notes, artificialanalysis.ai mobile board, published benchmarks through April 2026.
objective: Track the shape of local AI on phones and personal devices — which models run where, how capable they are at what size, how fast they serve on which silicon, and how the three platform stacks (Apple, Google, Qualcomm) and open-weight alternatives are diverging. Surface when a capability threshold is crossed that makes a new class of offline use cases viable.
successCondition: A reader opening the doc at the end of any monitoring period knows which on-device models matter this quarter, how the capability envelope on a flagship phone has moved, what platform integrations shipped, and which threshold crossings turned a previously-cloud workload into an on-device one.

objectiveFacets.name: Methodology declared
objectiveFacets.description: Device tiers, model-size axes, and phone-class benchmark choices are declared in a decision-record at the top of the doc.

objectiveFacets.name: Models tracked
objectiveFacets.description: Every on-device-first model family carries a current profile: size variants, license, platform support, benchmark claims.

objectiveFacets.name: Runtimes tracked
objectiveFacets.description: Every tracked runtime carries a capability-surface card covering which model formats it loads, which silicon it targets, and its current state.

objectiveFacets.name: Platforms tracked
objectiveFacets.description: Apple Intelligence, Android AICore, and Qualcomm AI Engine each carry a competitor-stance card with their current bet.

objectiveFacets.name: Indicators current
objectiveFacets.description: Capability-at-size, tokens/sec on specific reference phones, and adoption indicators carry latestValue + asOf + source.

objectiveFacets.name: Period attribution
objectiveFacets.description: Every update is attributable to a named monitoring period.

periods.summary: Opening baseline. Eight model families placed (Gemma 4 E-variants, Apple Foundation Models, Gemini Nano, Llama 3.2 mobile, Qwen 2.5 small, Phi mini, Granite 3, DeepSeek small). Six runtimes catalogued. Three platform stacks profiled. Four strategic moves logged. Seven indicators dashboarded. Baseline for capability-at-phone-size before quarterly cadence begins.

invariants.name: Methodology cites a choice
invariants.statement: The methodology decision-record must declare device tiers, model-size axes, and which benchmarks-at-size are in force, with rationale for each.

invariants.name: Every model card states the on-device variant's parameter count
invariants.statement: Every model-family card must name the specific variant(s) that run on-device and their parameter counts, since 'on-device' is size-dependent.

invariants.name: Every runtime names its silicon targets
invariants.statement: Every runtime card must name which silicon families it targets (Apple Silicon, Qualcomm Snapdragon, Google Tensor, Nvidia Jetson, generic CPU).

invariants.name: Every move has a dateOf and byActor
invariants.statement: Every strategic-move-log card must carry a dateOf and a byCompany reference.

invariants.name: Every indicator is dated with device context
invariants.statement: Every indicator card must have a latestValue with an as-of date, a source, and where the measurement is on a specific device, the device identified.

invariants.name: Every source has a publication date
invariants.statement: Every citation card must carry a publishedAt field.

callouts.title: What this tracker is and isn't
items: Tracks what runs on the device. Hybrid flows where the on-device model hands off to a cloud model (Apple Intelligence's Private Cloud Compute, Android System Intelligence) are noted but the cloud leg is out of scope — that's #105's territory.

Phone-class benchmarks are directional. MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval at under-4B parameter sizes are comparison points, not verdicts — small-model scores carry noise and format-sensitivity the dossier-prose should acknowledge.

Battery-per-token is a tracked indicator class, not a declared headline axis — measurements are workload- and temperature-dependent and we report what public sources publish rather than standardising.
Quarterly cadence: platform releases and flagship-phone hardware cycles move on quarter-to-year timescales. Weekly would fire on noise.

sections.title: Methodology in force (2026-H1)
sections.rationale: Device tiers, model-size axes, and phone-class benchmark choices declared up front. Changes are new decision-record entries, not edits — the prior methodology stays readable so a later reader knows what a given period's numbers actually compared.

data.name: Device tiers: flagship phone, mid-range phone, tablet / laptop, embedded

notes.text: Four device tiers tracked. **Flagship phone** = iPhone 15 Pro / 16 / 16 Pro class, Pixel 9 Pro / 10, Samsung S24 / S25 Ultra — Neural Engine class silicon, 8 GB+ RAM. **Mid-range phone** = iPhone 15, Pixel 9, Samsung A-series flagship — older NPU, 6–8 GB RAM. **Tablet / laptop** = iPad Pro M-series, MacBook Air M-series, Windows Copilot+ PC with NPU. **Embedded** = Raspberry Pi 5, Jetson Orin, Nintendo Switch — community runtime territory.

notes.title: Reference devices for indicators
items: Flagship phone reference: iPhone 15 Pro (Apple A17 Pro, 8 GB RAM, 16-core Neural Engine).
Mid-range phone reference: Pixel 9 (Tensor G4, 12 GB RAM, TPU/NPU).
Laptop reference: MacBook Air M3 (24 GB RAM).
Tokens/sec and TTFT are reported on the reference device where the measurement exists publicly; otherwise prose says 'reported on flagship phone class'.

data.name: Model-size axes: sub-2B phone-class, 2–8B flagship-phone / laptop, 8B+ laptop-class

notes.text: Three size tiers for on-device deployment. **Sub-2B** = fits comfortably on a mid-range phone (examples: Gemma 4 E2B, Llama 3.2 1B, Qwen 2.5 1.5B, Phi-3.5 mini depending on quantisation). **2–8B** = flagship phone or any laptop (Gemma 4 E4B, Llama 3.2 3B, Qwen 2.5 3B/7B, Phi-4 mini, Gemini Nano 3.25B). **8B+** = laptop-class on consumer hardware (Gemma 4 26B MoE and 31B dense — laptop-only per Google's own framing). Quantisation tier typically 4-bit (Q4_K_M or MLX 4-bit) for phone deployment; 8-bit or fp16 on laptop.

data.name: Capability axes: MMLU + GSM8K + HumanEval at size, plus platform-native tasks

notes.text: Three reference benchmarks tracked at size. **MMLU** for general knowledge — well-understood noise floor at small sizes. **GSM8K** for grade-school math — discriminates sub-2B models more than MMLU does. **HumanEval** for coding — the workload most builders justify on-device with. Platform-native tasks (iOS Writing Tools quality, Android Smart Reply quality, live transcription latency) are noted in prose but not tracked as primary axes — no cross-platform comparability.

notes.title: Small-model benchmark caveats
items: Format sensitivity is high at sub-2B: the same model can swing 10 points on MMLU depending on prompt formatting.
Quantisation hit varies by benchmark: GSM8K tends to lose more to 4-bit quantisation than MMLU does.
Lab-published numbers sometimes use different quantisation than what actually ships on device — cite the measurement conditions.
Rotation candidate for 2026-Q3 if a phone-class model exceeds 80% on any of the three: introduce a harder small-model reference (e.g. MMLU-Pro at size, or BIG-Bench Hard subset).

sections.title: Tracked on-device model families
sections.rationale: Eight model families tracked by their on-device variants. Models are the actors here, not labs, because on-device deployment is size- and architecture-specific — Llama 4's 31B doesn't run on a phone even though Meta ships it.

data.name: Gemma 4 E2B / E4B (Google · open-weight)
data.stage: Released 2026-04-02 under Apache 2.0; E-variants use Per-Layer Embeddings architecture
data.geography: Global via Hugging Face, llama.cpp, MLX, Ollama, day-zero multi-provider
data.strategicPosture: Open-weight phone-first with a novel on-device architecture
data.currentBet: Design a phone-shaped model from the ground up: Per-Layer Embeddings keep two billion parameters on the GPU while the rest lives in CPU or disk as a lookup table. Four sizes (E2B, E4B, 26B MoE, 31B dense) mean the same family ships from phone to laptop. Apache 2.0 license removes the licensing friction Gemma 3 carried. Demonstrated running on Android, iOS, Raspberry Pi, and Nintendo Switch (via community llama.cpp).
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — new family this period, 18 days of in-period data since April 2 launch.
activeInitiatives: E2B / E4B for phone-class on-device inference
Per-Layer Embeddings architecture for CPU/disk offload
Day-zero integration with Unsloth, MLX, Ollama, Hugging Face, vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp
Multimodal support (image, audio) in the E-variants
140+ language support via Gemini-derived tokenizer

notes.text: Omar Sanseviero (Google DeepMind), AI Engineer 2026-04, and Google Gemma 4 launch materials.

data.name: Apple Foundation Models (Apple · closed-weight · platform-integrated)
data.sector: Closed-weight · platform-integrated · on-device-first with cloud handoff
data.stage: On-device Foundation Models API available to developers as of iOS 19 / macOS 15; on-device model ~3B parameters
data.geography: iOS 18+ / macOS 15+ on Apple Silicon devices (iPhone 15 Pro, 16 line, M-series Macs and iPads)
data.strategicPosture: Platform-locked on-device with Private Cloud Compute as the hybrid escape valve
data.currentBet: Ship a small on-device Foundation Model as a platform primitive — tight integration with iOS Writing Tools, Siri, Shortcuts, and developer APIs. When the on-device model can't handle a request, hand off to Private Cloud Compute (Apple-controlled data centers running Apple Silicon). Vertical integration from silicon to OS to model is the bet; neither Google nor Qualcomm can match it at the same layer.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — captured as of 2026-04 with iOS 19 developer API expansions continuing.
activeInitiatives: On-device Foundation Models API for third-party developers
Private Cloud Compute for hybrid flows
Silicon-model co-design across Neural Engine generations
Writing Tools, Smart Reply, and Siri intelligence integration

notes.text: Apple developer documentation and WWDC 2024/2025 materials; iOS 19 release notes.

data.name: Gemini Nano (Google · closed-weight · Android-integrated)
data.stage: Gemini Nano 2 (~3.25B class) in System Core on Pixel 9/10 and Samsung S24/S25; accessible via Android AICore APIs
data.geography: Android flagship devices with AICore support; expanding through mid-range via vendor integration
data.strategicPosture: Platform primitive in Android via AICore, parallel to Apple's approach
data.currentBet: Distribute Gemini Nano through AICore as the default on-device model for Android, giving app developers a consistent API across Pixel and Samsung without bundling a model per app. Differentiates from Gemma on license and distribution: Nano is closed-weight and platform-bundled; Gemma is open-weight and anyone can host it.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — captured as of 2026-04 with AICore expansion through vendor partners continuing.
activeInitiatives: AICore distribution across Pixel and Samsung
System Core integration for zero-install app usage
Gemini Nano capability updates paired with Android releases

notes.text: Android Developers blog on AICore; Pixel 10 and Samsung S25 launch materials.

data.name: Llama 3.2 1B / 3B (Meta · open-weight · mobile-first variants)
data.stage: Released late 2025 as the mobile-first sizes of the Llama 3.2 family; widely hosted and fine-tuned
data.geography: Global via Hugging Face, llama.cpp, Ollama, ExecuTorch (Meta's own mobile runtime)
data.strategicPosture: Open-weight ecosystem play at phone scale
data.currentBet: Ship 1B and 3B sizes with first-class mobile targeting; use ExecuTorch as the reference mobile runtime. Bet is that open-weight plus a Meta-shepherded mobile runtime produces a credible third pole to Apple and Google's platform-locked stacks.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Carried from prior period. Llama 4's smallest variants compete in the 2–8B tier; 1B/3B remain the current phone-class story.
activeInitiatives: ExecuTorch as the reference mobile runtime
Fine-tuning communities around the 1B / 3B sizes
Multimodal variants in the pipeline

notes.text: Meta Llama release notes and ExecuTorch documentation, late 2025.

data.name: Qwen 2.5 0.5B / 1.5B / 3B / 7B (Alibaba · open-weight)
data.stage: Qwen 2.5 family released 2024–2025; small sizes remain state-of-the-art in the open-weight phone tier for many tasks
data.geography: Global via Hugging Face, Ollama, llama.cpp; particularly strong in Asian-language use cases
data.strategicPosture: Open-weight leader at small-model size, especially for multilingual workloads
data.currentBet: Keep shipping small models that punch above their weight class on reasoning and multilingual tasks. Qwen's 0.5B and 1.5B variants are the reference for sub-2B open-weight models in 2026.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Carried — next-gen Qwen 3 small sizes are anticipated but not confirmed for 2026-H1.
activeInitiatives: Qwen 2.5 small-size ecosystem
Multilingual strength as a positioning lever vs. Western-trained models
Community fine-tunes for vertical use cases

notes.text: Alibaba Qwen 2.5 release materials; open-weight leaderboard aggregators.

data.name: Phi-3.5 / Phi-4 mini (Microsoft · open-weight)
data.stage: Phi-4 mini (~3.8B) current reference; Phi-3.5 still widely deployed
data.geography: Global via Hugging Face, ONNX Runtime Mobile, llama.cpp; Microsoft uses them in Copilot+ PC scenarios
data.strategicPosture: Small-model research specialist + Copilot+ PC integration
data.currentBet: Build small models that match larger competitors' capabilities through better training data (textbook-quality, synthetic). Deploy into Copilot+ PC scenarios as the on-device AI primitive for Windows. Research-line openness (published papers, open weights) differentiates from closed-platform approaches.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Carried; Phi-4 mini released late 2025, remains the current reference.
activeInitiatives: Copilot+ PC integration (Windows NPU-accelerated)
Phi-4 mini as the on-device Copilot engine
ONNX Runtime Mobile as the reference runtime

notes.text: Microsoft Phi model card and Copilot+ PC documentation.

data.name: Granite 3 small (IBM · open-weight)
data.stage: Granite 3 family released 2024–2025 with 2B / 8B sizes prominent
data.strategicPosture: Enterprise-targeted open-weight small models with explicit provenance
data.currentBet: Serve enterprise on-device / on-premises use cases where model provenance, training-data transparency, and commercial indemnification matter. Less capable than frontier open-weight at comparable size but carries a compliance story few others match.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Carried; position specialised rather than growing rapidly.
activeInitiatives: Enterprise-tier indemnification and data provenance
Watsonx integration for hybrid deployment

notes.text: IBM Granite model cards and watsonx announcements.

data.name: DeepSeek V3 small variants (DeepSeek · open-weight)
data.stage: DeepSeek V3.2 released Q1 2026 at frontier scale; smaller distilled variants for on-device beginning to appear in the community
data.geography: Global via Hugging Face; China-origin lab
data.strategicPosture: Cost-leader reputation carrying into smaller sizes through community distillation
data.currentBet: V3 itself is not a phone model, but the community distilled small variants (V3 7B, distilled V3-derived 2B/3B) ride the DeepSeek reputation into the on-device tier. Less Meta-polished than Llama small sizes but competitive on reasoning benchmarks.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — V3.2 itself is carried from #105; the small-size story on-device is a late-2025 community development.
activeInitiatives: Community distillations in the 2–3B range
Chinese-market on-device deployments

notes.text: DeepSeek V3 release notes; Hugging Face community distillations.

sections.title: Tracked runtimes
sections.rationale: Six runtimes that make on-device deployment work. Each is a capability-surface card covering which silicon it targets, which model formats it loads, and its current state. Reused shape from ai-eval-ecosystem's frameworks section.

data.name: llama.cpp (universal)
data.purpose: Reference implementation for running quantised GGUF models on CPU, Apple Silicon (Metal), Nvidia (CUDA), and just about everything else. The lingua franca of on-device inference.

notes.text: llama.cpp GitHub; ggml.ai. Demonstrated on Nintendo Switch and Raspberry Pi in 2026.

data.name: MLX (Apple Silicon)
data.purpose: Apple-backed array framework and model runtime optimised for Apple Silicon's unified memory architecture. Fast, Python-friendly, first-class for Macs and increasingly iOS.

notes.text: MLX GitHub; Apple ML research publications.

data.name: Ollama
data.purpose: Developer-facing wrapper over llama.cpp with a server, CLI, and simple model-pull semantics. The default 'just try this model locally' experience for most developers in 2026.

notes.text: ollama.ai documentation.

data.name: Core ML (Apple platform)
data.purpose: Apple's framework for shipping ML models inside iOS / macOS apps. The official path for third-party developers to bundle a custom model into an app for on-device inference.

notes.text: Apple Core ML developer documentation.

data.name: Android AICore / Gemini Nano APIs
data.purpose: Android platform APIs that expose Gemini Nano as a system-provided service, so apps don't ship their own model. Combines a discovery + permissions layer with inference.

notes.text: Android Developers documentation on AICore.

data.name: ExecuTorch (Meta mobile runtime)
data.purpose: Meta's PyTorch-lineage mobile runtime for deploying Llama and other models on iOS and Android. The reference runtime for Llama 3.2 mobile sizes.

notes.text: ExecuTorch GitHub and Meta AI blog.

sections.title: Tracked platform stacks
sections.rationale: Three platform stacks as a distinct actor class from models and runtimes. Their competitive dynamic (vertical integration, app-developer reach, silicon leverage) is different from either.

data.name: Apple Intelligence
data.stage: Shipped in iOS 18 (2024), expanded through iOS 19 (2025–2026); Foundation Models API available to developers
data.geography: Apple-silicon iOS / macOS devices, Apple-controlled Private Cloud Compute for hybrid
data.strategicPosture: Vertically integrated on-device AI — silicon, OS, model, runtime, API, cloud-handoff all Apple-controlled
data.currentBet: Deliver on-device AI as a platform primitive with Private Cloud Compute as the escape valve for larger requests. Privacy as a default, not a feature. Developer opt-in via Foundation Models API, gated to Apple Silicon devices. The bet is that end-to-end control beats any single-layer advantage from competitors.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — Foundation Models API expansion tracked.
activeInitiatives: Foundation Models API GA for third-party developers
Private Cloud Compute expansion
Writing Tools, Siri, and Shortcuts intelligence updates
Silicon co-design (A17 Pro, A18, M3/M4 Neural Engines)

notes.text: Apple Intelligence launch materials and iOS 18/19 release notes.

data.name: Android AICore (Google)
data.sector: Platform stack · API-layer integration across vendors
data.stage: AICore available on Pixel 8+ from launch, expanded to Samsung S24+ through Google-Samsung partnership; supported by Qualcomm silicon partners
data.geography: Android flagships across Google, Samsung, and supported Qualcomm-powered OEMs
data.strategicPosture: API-layer standardisation for on-device AI across Android vendors
data.currentBet: Rather than vertically integrating like Apple, run an API-layer standard that any Android OEM can plug into via Qualcomm silicon or Tensor. Gemini Nano is the default model behind AICore, but the layer abstracts the underlying compute. The bet is that ecosystem breadth beats vertical depth — more apps, more devices, more Gemini Nano usage.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — Pixel 10 and S25 AICore expansions captured.
activeInitiatives: AICore expansion to more OEMs
Gemini Nano 2 deployment in System Core
Developer tools for AICore consumption

notes.text: Android Developers AICore documentation; Pixel 10 / S25 launch materials.

data.name: Qualcomm AI Engine (Snapdragon 8 Gen / X)
data.stage: Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 / 4 in flagship Androids; Snapdragon X Elite / Plus in Copilot+ PCs
data.geography: Qualcomm-powered Android flagships globally, plus Windows-on-Arm Copilot+ PCs
data.strategicPosture: Silicon-layer play that underwrites both the Android AI story and the Windows Copilot+ story
data.currentBet: Provide the NPU + AI Engine SDK that both Android AICore and Windows Copilot+ PCs run on top of. Qualcomm sits at the silicon layer and profits regardless of whether Google's AICore or Microsoft's Copilot wins the app layer above. The bet is on the inevitability of NPU-accelerated workloads.
data.evolutionSinceLastPeriod: Baseline — Snapdragon X Elite / Plus footprint in Copilot+ PCs captured.
activeInitiatives: Snapdragon 8 Gen 4 / 5 NPU generations
Snapdragon X mobile for Windows-on-Arm laptops
AI Engine SDK for developer targeting

notes.text: Qualcomm developer documentation and Snapdragon launch materials.

sections.title: On-device moves this period
sections.rationale: Discrete actions by labs and platforms that moved the on-device picture in 2026-H1. Launches, platform expansions, runtime milestones.

data.name: Google ships Gemma 4 E-variants — purpose-built for on-device
data.intent: Ship a phone-first open-weight model family under Apache 2.0, with Per-Layer Embeddings as the architectural innovation that makes two-billion-effective-parameter operation possible within phone memory budgets. Position Google as the only lab running closed-frontier (Gemini) + open-weight (Gemma) + platform-integrated (Gemini Nano in AICore) simultaneously.
data.observedEffect: Demonstrated running on iPhone, Android, Raspberry Pi, and Nintendo Switch within days of launch. 10M base-model downloads in the first 7 days. Day-zero integration across Unsloth, MLX, Ollama, HF, vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp. Threshold-crossing for what 'phone model' means — independent verification of 84.3% GPQA on the 31B variant still pending but the E-variants' on-device viability is demonstrated, not claimed.

notes.text: Omar Sanseviero at AI Engineer 2026-04; Google Gemma 4 launch materials.

data.name: Apple expands Foundation Models API to third-party developers
data.intent: Open the on-device Foundation Models API beyond Apple's first-party uses (Writing Tools, Siri) to third-party developers, letting apps call the platform model without bundling their own. Position Apple Intelligence as the on-device AI primitive on iOS.
data.observedEffect: Third-party adoption ramping through iOS 19 cycle; Private Cloud Compute handoff continues to cover requests the on-device model can't handle. Apple Silicon lock-in preserved — API not portable.

notes.text: Apple developer documentation; iOS 19 release notes.

data.name: Android AICore expands to Samsung S25 and broader Android flagship footprint
data.intent: Extend Gemini Nano availability from Pixel-only to the Samsung S25 flagship line via Samsung's System Core integration, widening the installed base that app developers can target through AICore.
data.observedEffect: AICore-supporting device footprint crosses the threshold where app developers can reasonably target Android on-device AI as a feature rather than a bonus. Qualcomm silicon as the shared substrate.

notes.text: Samsung S25 launch materials; Android Developers AICore documentation.

data.name: Llama 3.2 1B/3B reach production-deployment maturity on ExecuTorch
data.intent: ExecuTorch + Llama 3.2 1B/3B cross from 'demo-friendly' to 'production-friendly' for mobile app developers shipping on-device features. Meta-shepherded open-weight stack as a third pole to Apple and Google platform-locked approaches.
data.observedEffect: Growing pool of third-party apps shipping Llama 3.2 for on-device features via ExecuTorch; community documentation and example code crossing the threshold that lets non-specialist developers integrate.

notes.text: ExecuTorch GitHub and Meta AI engineering blog.

sections.title: Capability indicators
sections.rationale: What the best on-device model can do at phone size. Top score tracked per benchmark in the current methodology. Direction of travel and saturation posture on each.

data.name: Top MMLU at under-4B parameter size (phone-class)
data.latestValue: Reported scores in the ~70–75% range from the current wave of phone-class models (Gemma 4 E4B, Phi-4 mini, Qwen 2.5 3B at comparable quantisation). A year ago this band sat around 60%.
data.asOf: 2026-04-21 · public lab claims and Hugging Face leaderboard aggregators
data.forecast: Continued rise if Gemma 4 E-variants' architecture approach generalises; threshold-crossing toward 80% plausible within 12 months.

notes.text: Lab-published scores for Gemma 4, Phi-4 mini, Qwen 2.5 3B, late 2025 / Q1 2026.

data.name: Top HumanEval at under-4B size (phone-class coding)
data.latestValue: Frontier phone-class coding sits around 55–65% HumanEval for the best sub-4B open-weight and closed models; Gemma 4 E4B reported near the top of the band.
data.asOf: 2026-04-21 · public claims and community benchmark runs
data.forecast: Coding at size is a noisier benchmark than knowledge; small gains over the period but less threshold-crossing than MMLU.

notes.text: Model-card claims for phone-class variants, Q1 2026.

data.name: On-device multimodal capability breadth
data.latestValue: Gemma 4 E-variants ship with image, audio, and speech-to-translate multimodal support out of the box. Apple Foundation Models and Gemini Nano support image understanding. Llama 3.2 1B / 3B are text-only in their mobile-first variants.
data.forecast: Multimodal becoming table stakes for the flagship on-device tier within the next 12 months; text-only will read as a capability gap.

notes.text: Model cards for each tracked family.

sections.title: Latency indicators (device-specific)
sections.rationale: Tokens-per-second and cold-start on the reference devices declared in methodology. These are the numbers a builder actually pays attention to when deciding whether a workload is viable offline.

data.name: Output tokens/sec at phone-class size on flagship phone
data.latestValue: Reported on flagship phones (iPhone 15 Pro, Pixel 9 Pro): ~20–40 tok/s at sub-2B quantised (Gemma 4 E2B, Llama 3.2 1B). ~8–15 tok/s at 2–4B quantised (Gemma 4 E4B, Phi-4 mini). Numbers vary with quantisation and runtime.
data.asOf: 2026-04-21 · public reports from Ollama / MLX / llama.cpp community benchmarks
data.forecast: Rising with silicon-generation cycle (A18, Tensor G5); quantisation-aware training pushes the floor up.

notes.text: Community benchmark reports on Ollama, MLX, and ExecuTorch forums, Q1 2026.

data.name: Cold-start time to first token on flagship phone
data.latestValue: Typical cold-start: ~500ms–1.5s depending on model size and whether the runtime is already warmed. Apple Intelligence and AICore use system-level warm state to reduce this for platform-integrated models.
data.forecast: Platform-integrated stacks will keep their TTFT advantage over bundled-model approaches via warm-state preservation.

notes.text: Reported TTFT across community benchmark runs, Q1 2026.

sections.title: Adoption indicators
sections.rationale: Downloads, app-count, platform-enablement. The signals that tell you whether on-device is a hobbyist curiosity or a real deployment target.

data.name: Open-weight mobile adoption velocity (Gemma 4 reference)
data.latestValue: Gemma 4 base models crossed 10M downloads in the first 7 days (cross-referenced with #105). 500M+ lifetime Gemma family. 100K+ community models, 1000+ on Gemma 4 specifically in week one.
data.forecast: Sustained open-weight mobile adoption if E-variants continue to prove usable on real phones; community fine-tunes add the long tail.

notes.text: Omar Sanseviero (Google DeepMind), AI Engineer conference 2026-04.

data.name: Apple Intelligence enablement on eligible devices
data.latestValue: Directional: Apple Intelligence is enabled on a rising share of iOS 18+ / 19 eligible devices, with Foundation Models API usage now extending to third-party apps. Precise enablement rate not publicly disclosed.
data.forecast: Rising as the API footprint grows and first-party features become more integrated into daily iOS use.

notes.text: Apple developer materials and iOS 19 release coverage.

data.name: Android AICore-supporting device footprint
data.latestValue: AICore present on Pixel 8+, Pixel 9, Pixel 10, Samsung S24, Samsung S25. Expanding through Qualcomm-silicon OEM partners. Cumulative installed base crossing into the hundreds of millions of devices through 2026.
data.forecast: Rising linearly with Android flagship-phone sales cycle; AICore app count grows with developer adoption.

notes.text: Android Developers AICore documentation; Pixel 10 and Samsung S25 launch materials.

sections.title: Position map — platform-locked × offline-only
sections.rationale: X-axis: platform-locked (closed, vertically integrated) ← → portable (runs anywhere). Y-axis: offline-only ← → hybrid (cloud handoff when needed). Prior position preserved per card; movement reveals as the next period runs.

data.name: Gemma 4 E2B / E4B
data.axisX: strongly portable (runs on any llama.cpp / MLX / Ollama target)

data.name: Phi-4 mini
data.axisX: portable but Windows-optimised via Copilot+ PC

sections.title: Period notes
sections.rationale: One card per monitoring period summarising what shifted, what's worth watching, and which dossier pieces need coordination.

notes.text: Opening baseline as of 2026-04-21. Methodology declared (four device tiers, three size axes, three reference benchmarks at size, saturation policy). Eight model families tracked, three with first-class on-device focus (Gemma 4 E-variants, Apple Foundation Models, Gemini Nano) and five portable open-weight (Llama 3.2 mobile, Qwen 2.5 small, Phi mini, Granite 3, DeepSeek small). Six runtimes catalogued across universal (llama.cpp, Ollama), platform (MLX, Core ML, AICore), and vendor (ExecuTorch). Three platform stacks profiled. Four moves logged. Seven indicators across capability, latency, adoption. Position map placed eight entities across platform-locked × offline-only axes. Cross-referenced with #105 (inference economics) on the Gemma 4 adoption signal.

notes.title: Focus for 2026-Q3
items: Does any phone-class (sub-4B) model cross 80% MMLU or GSM8K? Threshold-crossing would trigger benchmark rotation.
Does Llama 4's smallest variant ship with first-class mobile targeting (ExecuTorch integration)?
Does Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite 2 (or similar) ship with materially higher NPU performance?
Does Apple Foundation Models API adoption by third-party apps produce visible category winners (translation, summarisation, coding assistance)?
Does the Chinese open-weight mobile community (Qwen 3 small, DeepSeek next-gen small) ship phone-class entries that challenge the current reference set?
Cross-reference with #105: does Gemma 4 adoption continue to accelerate, and does the on-device signal diverge from the hosted-inference signal?
